steps.explain: `retry_on` only controls WHEN a job runs again — it says nothing about whether running it twice is safe. That safety has to come from the job's own logic: module 19's idempotency key on the ledger entry is what makes a second attempt a no-op instead of a second payout. Retries and idempotency are two different concerns that both have to be right.
steps.nudge: `retry_on` decides whether to run the job again. What decides whether running it again is actually safe?

md: ## Two structures do most of the work
Ninety percent of everyday backend code — and most interview problems — leans on exactly two structures: the **dynamic array** (Ruby's `Array`) and the **hash map** (Ruby's `Hash`).

Before the structures, one piece of vocabulary: **Big-O**. It describes how the work grows as the input `n` grows. `O(1)` means constant — the same cost whether you have 10 items or 10 million. `O(n)` means linear — twice the data, twice the work.
> The whole game is spotting when an `O(n)` scan can become an `O(1)` lookup.
## The array
An `Array` is a contiguous, index-addressable list. Two facts to memorize:

- `arr[i]` — reading by index is `O(1)`. Instant, at any size.
- `arr.push(x)` — appending to the end is `O(1)` *amortized* (occasionally Ruby grows the backing buffer, but averaged out it's constant).
The trap: **finding a value** in an array is `O(n)` — you scan every element until you hit it. `arr.include?(x)` and `arr.find { ... }` are both linear.

steps.title: playground/find_booking.rb
steps.source:
bookings = [
  { id: 10, dog: "Mochi",  status: "booked" },
  { id: 11, dog: "Cooper", status: "walking" },
  { id: 12, dog: "Bella",  status: "done" },
]

# O(n): scan the whole array to find one booking
found = bookings.find { |b| b[:id] == 11 }
puts found[:dog]  # => Cooper
steps.caption: Fine for 3 bookings. For 3 million, every lookup walks the list.

md: ## The hash map
A `Hash` maps keys to values with `O(1)` **average** lookup, insert, and delete. It hashes the key to jump straight to the slot — no scanning.
Reshape the bookings into a by-id hash and lookups stop depending on `n` entirely.

steps.title: playground/booking_map.rb
steps.source:
by_id = {
  10 => { dog: "Mochi",  status: "booked" },
  11 => { dog: "Cooper", status: "walking" },
  12 => { dog: "Bella",  status: "done" },
}

# O(1) average: hash straight to the slot, no scan
puts by_id[11][:dog]  # => Cooper
steps.caption: Build the index once, then every lookup is constant time.

md: ## Worked problem: Two Sum
The classic. *Given an array of numbers and a target, return the indices of the two numbers that add to the target.*

The brute force is two nested loops — `O(n^2)`. The hash-map insight: as you walk the array, for each number `x` you need its complement `target - x`. Store every value you've seen in a `seen` hash mapping value to its index. If the complement is already in `seen`, you're done — one pass, `O(n)`.

steps.title: playground/two_sum_idea.rb
steps.source:
def two_sum(nums, target)
  seen = {}                       # value => index
  nums.each_with_index do |x, i|
    need = target - x
    return [seen[need], i] if seen.key?(need)
    seen[x] = i
  end
  nil
end

p two_sum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9)  # => [0, 1]
steps.caption: One pass, O(n) time, O(n) space. The map turns a scan into a lookup.

steps.q: You look up a booking by id 5,000 times per second. Which structure gives O(1) average lookup?
choices: An Array, scanned with .find
A Hash keyed by id
A sorted Array
It makes no difference
steps.explain: A Hash keyed by id hashes straight to the slot — O(1) average — while Array#find is an O(n) scan every call.
steps.nudge: Which one avoids walking the whole list on every lookup?

steps.title: Two Sum with a hash map
prompt: Implement two_sum(nums, target). Walk nums with each_with_index. For each x, compute need = target - x, and if seen already has need, return [seen[need], i]. Otherwise store seen[x] = i.
Fill in the two missing lines where the marker is.
steps.starter:
def two_sum(nums, target)
  seen = {}
  nums.each_with_index do |x, i|
    need = target - x
    # your code here
  end
  nil
end

checks.hint: Compute the complement: need = target - x.
Return the stored index for the complement: seen[need].
Remember this value's index: seen[x] = i.

mustNot.hint: One pass only — no nested loop. The map is what removes the second loop.

steps.success: One pass, O(n). You traded a nested scan for a hash lookup — the core trick behind half of LeetCode.

md: ## LIFO and FIFO
A **stack** is last-in-first-out (LIFO): the last thing you pushed is the first thing you pop. Think of an undo history — the most recent action undoes first.
A **queue** is first-in-first-out (FIFO): the first thing in is the first thing out. Think of a job queue — the oldest job runs next.
Both are just disciplined ways of adding and removing from a list. What matters is the cost of those operations.
## Ruby: the honest picture
For a **stack**, a plain `Array` is perfect: `push` and `pop` both hit the end and are `O(1)`.

For a **queue**, the naive move is `push` to the end and `shift` from the front — but `Array#shift` is `O(n)`, because every remaining element slides down one slot. Fine for a handful of items; a real bottleneck at scale.

> For a genuine concurrent work queue in Ruby, reach for `Thread::Queue` (aliased `Queue`) from the stdlib — it's built for producer/consumer handoff. (Python's equivalent is `collections.deque`, which gives O(1) at both ends.)

steps.title: playground/undo_stack.rb
steps.source:
undo = []
undo.push("assign walker")   # push: O(1)
undo.push("start walk")
undo.push("send GPS fix")

undo.pop   # => "send GPS fix"  (LIFO), O(1)
undo.pop   # => "start walk"
steps.caption: Array#push / #pop are an O(1) stack. Last in, first out.

md: ## Worked problem: Valid Parentheses
*Given a string of brackets `()[]{}`, is every bracket correctly closed and nested?*

A stack is the natural fit. Walk the string: push every opener. On a closer, the top of the stack must be its matching opener — pop and compare. If it doesn't match (or the stack is empty), it's invalid. At the end the stack must be empty.
Map each closer to the opener it expects, so the check is one hash lookup. `O(n)` time.

steps.title: playground/valid_parens.rb
steps.source:
def valid_parentheses(s)
  pairs = { ")" => "(", "]" => "[", "}" => "{" }
  stack = []
  s.each_char do |c|
    if pairs.key?(c)
      return false if stack.pop != pairs[c]
    else
      stack.push(c)
    end
  end
  stack.empty?
end

p valid_parentheses("([]{})")  # => true
p valid_parentheses("(]")      # => false
steps.caption: Closers pop and must match; leftover openers mean unbalanced.

steps.q: Why is a Ruby queue built from Array#push + Array#shift slow at scale?
choices: push is O(n)
shift is O(n) — every element slides down one slot
Arrays can't hold enough items
shift reverses the array
steps.explain: shift removes from the front, so all remaining elements move down one index — O(n) per call. Use Thread::Queue (Ruby) or deque (Python) for O(1) dequeue.
steps.nudge: Removing from the front means everything behind it has to move.

steps.title: Valid Parentheses with a stack
prompt: Finish valid_parentheses(s). On a closer c (a key in pairs), the popped opener must equal pairs[c] — otherwise return false. On an opener, push it.
Fill in the two branches at the marker.
steps.starter:
def valid_parentheses(s)
  pairs = { ")" => "(", "]" => "[", "}" => "{" }
  stack = []
  s.each_char do |c|
    if pairs.key?(c)
      # your code here
    else
      # your code here
    end
  end
  stack.empty?
end
steps.solution:
def valid_parentheses(s)
  pairs = { ")" => "(", "]" => "[", "}" => "{" }
  stack = []
  s.each_char do |c|
    if pairs.key?(c)
      return false if stack.pop != pairs[c]
    else
      stack.push(c)
    end
  end
  stack.empty?
end

checks.hint: On a closer: return false if stack.pop != pairs[c].
On an opener: stack.push(c).

mustNot.hint: Don't delete the final stack.empty? — that's the balanced check.

steps.success: The stack remembered nesting order for you. Leftover openers => unbalanced.

md: ## Nodes joined by pointers

An array stores everything in one contiguous block. A **linked list** stores each value in its own **node**, and each node holds a `next` pointer to the following node. Follow the chain from the `head` until `next` is `nil`.
The whole trade-off comes from that indirection.
## When they win, when they lose
**Win:** given a node, inserting or removing right there is `O(1)` — you just rewire a couple of `next` pointers. No shifting a million elements like an array insert would.

**Lose:** there's no `list[i]`. To reach the 500th node you follow 500 pointers — random access is `O(n)`. And because nodes are scattered across memory, they have poor **cache locality**, so even sequential walks are slower per-step than an array's tight loop.

> In practice you rarely hand-roll one in Ruby — `Array` wins for most work. But linked lists are interview bread-and-butter, and they're how real queues, LRU caches, and adjacency lists are built underneath.

steps.title: playground/node.rb
steps.source:
class ListNode
  attr_accessor :val, :next
  def initialize(val, nxt = nil)
    @val = val
    @next = nxt
  end
end

# 1 -> 2 -> 3
head = ListNode.new(1, ListNode.new(2, ListNode.new(3)))

node = head
while node
  print node.val, " "   # => 1 2 3
  node = node.next
end
steps.caption: A node is a value plus a next pointer. Walk it until next is nil.

md: ## Worked problem: reverse a linked list
*Reverse a singly linked list, return the new head.*

The iterative trick uses three pointers: `prev` (starts `nil`), `curr` (starts at `head`), and a temporary `nxt`. For each node: save `nxt = curr.next`, flip `curr.next = prev`, then slide both forward: `prev = curr`, `curr = nxt`. When `curr` is `nil`, `prev` is the new head.
One pass, `O(n)` time, `O(1)` extra space.

steps.title: playground/reverse.rb
steps.source:
def reverse_list(head)
  prev = nil
  curr = head
  while curr
    nxt = curr.next     # save the rest
    curr.next = prev    # flip the pointer
    prev = curr         # slide prev forward
    curr = nxt          # slide curr forward
  end
  prev                  # new head
end
steps.caption: Three pointers, one pass. prev ends up pointing at the reversed chain.

steps.q: What does a linked list give you that a plain array does not?
choices: O(1) random access by index
O(1) insert/remove at a known node
Better cache locality
Automatic sorting
steps.explain: Given a node, insert/remove is O(1) pointer rewiring. Arrays lose here (shifting), but arrays win on O(1) indexing and cache locality.
steps.nudge: Think about what an array has to do to insert in the middle.

steps.title: Reverse a linked list
prompt: Finish reverse_list(head). Inside the loop: save nxt = curr.next, set curr.next = prev, then advance prev = curr and curr = nxt.
Fill in the four lines at the marker.
steps.starter:
def reverse_list(head)
  prev = nil
  curr = head
  while curr
    # your code here
  end
  prev
end
steps.solution:
def reverse_list(head)
  prev = nil
  curr = head
  while curr
    nxt = curr.next
    curr.next = prev
    prev = curr
    curr = nxt
  end
  prev
end

checks.hint: Save the rest first: nxt = curr.next.
Flip the pointer: curr.next = prev.
Slide prev forward: prev = curr.
Slide curr forward: curr = nxt.

mustNot.hint: Rewire the pointers yourself — don't call a built-in reverse.

steps.success: Three pointers, one pass, O(1) extra space. prev is the new head.

md: ## A node with children

A **tree** is like a linked list that branches. A **binary tree** node holds a value and two child pointers, `left` and `right` (either may be `nil`). The top node is the **root**; a node with no children is a **leaf**.
Trees model anything hierarchical: a comment thread with replies, a category tree, a file system.
## Recursion is the natural tool

A tree is defined in terms of itself — each child is the root of a smaller tree. So the natural way to process one is **recursion**: do something with the node, then recurse into `left` and `right`. The base case is `nil` (an empty tree).
**Depth-first search (DFS)** comes in three orders by *when* you visit the node versus its children:

- **pre-order**: node, then left, then right.
- **in-order**: left, node, right.
- **post-order**: left, right, then node.
## The BST ordering property
A **binary search tree (BST)** adds one rule: for every node, everything in its `left` subtree is smaller and everything in its `right` subtree is larger.

That rule is what makes lookup fast: to find a value, compare and go left or right, halving the search space each step — `O(log n)` on a balanced tree. An **in-order** traversal of a BST visits values in sorted order, which is a handy thing to know.

steps.title: playground/tree.rb
steps.source:
class TreeNode
  attr_accessor :val, :left, :right
  def initialize(val, left = nil, right = nil)
    @val = val
    @left = left
    @right = right
  end
end

#     4
#    / \
#   2   6
root = TreeNode.new(4, TreeNode.new(2), TreeNode.new(6))

def inorder(node, out = [])
  return out if node.nil?
  inorder(node.left, out)   # left
  out << node.val           # node
  inorder(node.right, out)  # right
  out
end

p inorder(root)  # => [2, 4, 6]  (sorted, because it's a BST)
steps.caption: In-order on a BST yields sorted values. The nil check is the base case.

md: ## Worked problem: maximum depth
*Return the maximum depth (height) of a binary tree — the number of nodes on the longest root-to-leaf path.*
Pure recursion. An empty tree (`nil`) has depth `0`. Otherwise the depth is `1` (for the current node) plus the deeper of the two subtrees. `O(n)` — you touch each node once.

steps.title: playground/max_depth.rb
steps.source:
def max_depth(node)
  return 0 if node.nil?
  1 + [max_depth(node.left), max_depth(node.right)].max
end

p max_depth(root)  # => 2
steps.caption: Base case nil => 0. Otherwise 1 + the taller subtree.

steps.q: In a binary search tree, where do all values SMALLER than a node live?
choices: In its right subtree
In its left subtree
Only at leaves
They can be anywhere
steps.explain: The BST property: everything in the left subtree is smaller, everything in the right subtree is larger. That's what enables O(log n) search on a balanced tree.
steps.nudge: Smaller goes one way, larger the other — which side is conventionally smaller?

steps.title: Maximum depth of a binary tree
prompt: Implement max_depth(node). Base case: return 0 when node is nil. Otherwise return 1 plus the max of the left and right subtree depths.
Fill in the recursive line at the marker.
steps.starter:
def max_depth(node)
  return 0 if node.nil?
  # your code here
end
steps.solution:
def max_depth(node)
  return 0 if node.nil?
  1 + [max_depth(node.left), max_depth(node.right)].max
end

checks.hint: Base case: return 0 if node.nil?.
Recurse left: max_depth(node.left).
Recurse right: max_depth(node.right).
Add 1 for the current node.

steps.success: Recursion mirrors the tree's own shape — the base case at nil stops it.

md: ## Nodes and edges

A **graph** is nodes connected by edges — more general than a tree (cycles allowed, no single root). The most practical representation for problems is an **adjacency map**: a hash from each node to the list of its neighbors.
Anchor it in PawWalk: neighborhoods connected by walkable routes. Which neighborhoods can a walker reach on foot from where they are?

steps.title: playground/graph.rb
steps.source:
# neighborhood => directly walkable neighbors
routes = {
  "Downtown"   => ["Riverside", "Oakhill"],
  "Riverside"  => ["Downtown", "Maple"],
  "Oakhill"    => ["Downtown"],
  "Maple"      => ["Riverside"],
  "Isla"       => [],          # not connected to the rest
}
steps.caption: An adjacency map: each key points to its immediate neighbors.

md: ## Two ways to explore: DFS and BFS
**Depth-first search (DFS)** dives down one path as far as it goes before backtracking. It's natural with recursion, or with an explicit **stack**.
**Breadth-first search (BFS)** explores in rings — all immediate neighbors first, then their neighbors. It uses a **queue**, and it finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
The one thing both MUST have is a **visited set**. Graphs have cycles: without remembering where you've been, you'd loop forever between two connected nodes.
## Worked problem: who's reachable
*From a starting neighborhood, which ones can you reach?* This is a flood fill — the same routine behind "number of islands" and "connected components".

DFS with a stack: seed the stack with `start`. Pop a node; if unvisited, mark it and push all its neighbors. Repeat until the stack is empty. The visited set is your answer. `O(V + E)` — you touch each node and edge once.

steps.title: playground/reachable.rb
steps.source:
def reachable(graph, start)
  visited = []
  stack = [start]
  until stack.empty?
    node = stack.pop
    next if visited.include?(node)
    visited << node
    graph[node].each { |n| stack.push(n) }
  end
  visited
end

p reachable(routes, "Downtown")
# => ["Downtown", "Oakhill", "Riverside", "Maple"]  (Isla unreachable)
steps.caption: Stack-based DFS. The visited list is both the guard and the result.

steps.q: Why does every graph traversal need a visited set?
choices: To sort the nodes
To avoid infinite loops on cycles
To make lookups O(1)
It's optional for small graphs
steps.explain: Graphs can contain cycles. Without tracking visited nodes, the traversal revisits them forever. The visited set both prevents cycles and doubles as the reachable result.
steps.nudge: What happens when two nodes point back at each other?

steps.title: Reachable nodes via DFS
prompt: Finish reachable(graph, start). Pop a node; skip it if already visited; otherwise mark it visited and push each neighbor onto the stack.
Fill in the three lines at the marker.
steps.starter:
def reachable(graph, start)
  visited = []
  stack = [start]
  until stack.empty?
    node = stack.pop
    # your code here
  end
  visited
end
steps.solution:
def reachable(graph, start)
  visited = []
  stack = [start]
  until stack.empty?
    node = stack.pop
    next if visited.include?(node)
    visited << node
    graph[node].each { |n| stack.push(n) }
  end
  visited
end

checks.hint: Skip already-seen nodes: next if visited.include?(node).
Mark it visited: visited << node.
Visit neighbors: graph[node].each { ... }.
Push each neighbor: stack.push(n).

steps.success: The visited set stopped the cycles and became your answer. Swap the stack for a queue and it's BFS.

md: ## Always grab the best one, cheaply
A **priority queue** answers one question fast: *what's the smallest (or largest) item right now?* You keep pushing items in any order, and each pop hands you the current min (or max).
The structure underneath is a **heap** — a tree kept partly ordered so the min (or max) is always at the root. Push and pop are `O(log n)`; peeking at the min is `O(1)`.
Real uses: dispatch the *nearest available* walker first; run the *highest-priority* job before the rest.
## The honest language note
**Python** ships `heapq` — a min-heap built on a plain list (`heapq.heappush`, `heapq.heappop`). That's the standard tool there.
**Ruby has no heap in its standard library.** Say it plainly, because it trips people up. Your options:

- `Array#min(k)` / `Array#max(k)` return the smallest/largest `k` elements — great when you just need the top few.
- The `algorithms` gem provides a real `Containers::Heap`.
- Or hand-roll a binary heap (a good interview exercise).
> For the exercise below we'll use the built-in `Array#max(k)`, which is the pragmatic Ruby answer.

steps.title: playground/nearest_walker.rb
steps.source:
# distances in meters to available walkers
distances = [820, 140, 300, 60, 500]

# Ruby has no stdlib heap; min(k) returns the k smallest, sorted
nearest_three = distances.min(3)   # => [60, 140, 300]
puts "dispatch: #{nearest_three.first} m away"  # => 60 m away
steps.caption: Array#min(k) covers the common 'top few' case without a heap.

md: ## Worked problem: kth largest element
*Return the kth largest element in an array* (k = 1 is the maximum).

The heap-based approach keeps a min-heap of size `k`: push each number, and whenever the heap exceeds `k`, pop the smallest. The root is then the kth largest. That's `O(n log k)` time and `O(k)` space — better than sorting the whole array (`O(n log n)`) when `k` is small.
In Ruby, `nums.max(k)` returns the `k` largest in descending order, so `nums.max(k).last` is the kth largest directly.

steps.title: playground/kth_largest.rb
steps.source:
def kth_largest(nums, k)
  nums.max(k).last
end

p kth_largest([3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4], 2)  # => 5  (2nd largest)
steps.caption: max(k) gives the top k descending; .last is the kth. A true heap would be O(n log k).

steps.q: What's true about heaps in Ruby versus Python?
choices: Both ship a heap in the standard library
Python has heapq; Ruby has no stdlib heap (use max(k)/min(k) or a gem)
Ruby has heapq; Python does not
Neither language can implement a heap
steps.explain: Python's heapq is a stdlib min-heap on a list. Ruby has no stdlib heap — Array#max(k)/#min(k), the algorithms gem, or a hand-rolled binary heap fill the gap.
steps.nudge: One of these languages makes you reach outside the stdlib.

steps.title: Kth largest element
prompt: Implement kth_largest(nums, k) using the built-in: nums.max(k) returns the k largest in descending order, so the kth largest is the last of those.
Fill in the one line at the marker. (The true heap approach is O(n log k).)

checks.hint: Use nums.max(k).last — the k largest descending, then the last one.

mustNot.hint: Skip a full sort — max(k) does the top-k work directly.

steps.success: max(k).last is the pragmatic Ruby answer; a size-k min-heap would make it O(n log k).

md: ## The hash map is a swiss-army knife
You met the hash map in lesson one. This capstone is about the three *patterns* it unlocks — the ones that solve a huge fraction of string and array problems:

- **Frequency counts** — how many times each thing appears.
- **Grouping by a canonical key** — bucket items that share some computed key.
- **Seen-sets** — remember what you've encountered (Two Sum was this).
Recognizing which pattern a problem wants is most of the battle.
## The tools in each language
**Python** gives you `collections.Counter` (counts in one line) and `collections.defaultdict(list)` (a dict that auto-creates an empty list per key, so you can `d[key].append(x)` without checking).
**Ruby** gives you the same power idiomatically:

- `Hash.new(0)` — a hash with default `0`, so `counts[x] += 1` just works.
- `Array#tally` — counts occurrences in one call: `%w[a b a].tally` => `{ "a" => 2, "b" => 1 }`.
- `each_with_object` and `group_by` — accumulate into a hash or bucket by a computed key.

steps.title: playground/counts.rb
steps.caption: Hash.new(0) makes counting a one-liner; tally does it for you.

md: ## Worked problem: Group Anagrams
*Group words that are anagrams of each other* (same letters, reordered): `["eat","tea","ate","tan","nat"]` => `[["eat","tea","ate"],["tan","nat"]]`.

The insight is the **canonical key**: two words are anagrams exactly when their sorted letters match. So sort each word's characters to get a key, and bucket words under that key in a hash. One pass, `O(n * m log m)` for `n` words of length `m`.
`Hash.new { |h, k| h[k] = [] }` gives an auto-vivifying hash — index a missing key and it creates an empty array for you, so you can push straight away.

steps.title: playground/group_anagrams.rb
steps.source:
def group_anagrams(words)
  groups = Hash.new { |h, k| h[k] = [] }
  words.each do |w|
    key = w.chars.sort.join   # canonical key
    groups[key] << w
  end
  groups.values
end

p group_anagrams(%w[eat tea ate tan nat])
# => [["eat", "tea", "ate"], ["tan", "nat"]]
steps.caption: Sorted letters are the shared key; the hash buckets each word under it.

steps.q: What makes two words land in the same anagram group?
choices: They have the same length
Their letters, sorted, are identical
They start with the same letter
They hash to the same number by luck
steps.explain: Anagrams are the same multiset of letters. Sorting each word's characters produces a canonical key that's identical for all anagrams — that's the grouping key.
steps.nudge: How would you turn 'eat' and 'tea' into the exact same string?

steps.title: Group Anagrams
prompt: Finish group_anagrams(words). For each word w, build the canonical key with w.chars.sort.join, then push w into groups under that key.
Fill in the two lines at the marker; return groups.values.
steps.starter:
def group_anagrams(words)
  groups = Hash.new { |h, k| h[k] = [] }
  words.each do |w|
    # your code here
  end
  groups.values
end
steps.solution:
def group_anagrams(words)
  groups = Hash.new { |h, k| h[k] = [] }
  words.each do |w|
    key = w.chars.sort.join
    groups[key] << w
  end
  groups.values
end

checks.hint: Build the canonical key: key = w.chars.sort.join.
Bucket the word: groups[key] << w.

mustNot.hint: Return groups.values once — the buckets are already the answer.

steps.success: Sorted-letters as a key is the whole trick. That's the grouping pattern — reuse it anywhere a canonical key exists.

md: ## Singleton: one shared instance for the whole app
**Problem:** you want exactly *one* instance of something — a `Config`, a shared `Stripe` client, a logger — and one global point to reach it.
**PawWalk scenario:** app configuration loaded once at boot. Every part of the API reads the same `Config` object; nobody should build a second one.
Ruby ships the pattern in the stdlib: `require "singleton"; include Singleton` makes `.new` private and gives you `.instance`.
> Be honest: in Ruby a plain constant or a module of module-methods is *usually* enough. Reach for `Singleton` only when you need lazy init plus a real object with state.

steps.title: playground/singleton.rb
steps.source:
require "singleton"

class Config
  include Singleton
  attr_accessor :stripe_key, :base_url

  def initialize
    @stripe_key = ENV["STRIPE_KEY"]
    @base_url   = "https://api.pawwalk.dev"
  end
end

Config.instance.base_url            # => "https://api.pawwalk.dev"
Config.instance.equal?(Config.instance)  # => true (same object)
# Config.new                        # => NoMethodError: private method
steps.caption: include Singleton privatizes .new and memoizes one .instance.

steps.q: After include Singleton, what happens when you call Config.new?
choices: It raises NoMethodError — .new is now private
It returns the shared instance
It builds a fresh, separate Config
It raises FrozenError
steps.explain: Singleton makes .new private so the ONLY way in is Config.instance, guaranteeing one object.
steps.nudge: The whole point is that there is exactly one way to get the instance.

steps.title: Make Config a singleton
prompt: Add the two lines that turn Config into a singleton: require the stdlib and include the module.
steps.starter:
# your code here

class Config
  # include the module here
  attr_accessor :stripe_key
end
steps.solution:
require "singleton"

class Config
  include Singleton
  attr_accessor :stripe_key
end

checks.hint: Pull in the stdlib: require "singleton".
Inside the class: include Singleton.

mustNot.hint: Let the module supply .instance — don't hand-write it.

steps.success: Two lines and Config can never be duplicated.

md: ## Factory Method: a method picks the concrete class
**Problem:** callers should ask for *what* they want, not know *which* class builds it.
**PawWalk scenario:** a booking update goes out over email, SMS, or push. A single `notifier_for(channel)` returns the right notifier; the rest of the code just calls `.notify`.
In Ruby this is often a plain `case` in one method — no `Creator` subclass hierarchy needed. That IS the factory method.

steps.title: playground/factory_method.rb
steps.caption: One method decides the class; callers stay ignorant of it.

steps.q: What does the Factory Method pattern let callers avoid knowing?
choices: Which concrete class to instantiate
How many CPUs the host has
The Ruby version
Whether a block was given
steps.explain: Callers name the intent (a channel) and the factory maps it to a concrete class.
steps.nudge: It hides the 'which class' decision behind one method.

steps.title: Build the right notifier
prompt: Finish notifier_for so "email" returns EmailNotifier.new and anything else returns PushNotifier.new.
steps.solution:
def notifier_for(channel)
  if channel == "email"
    EmailNotifier.new
  else
    PushNotifier.new
  end
end

checks.hint: Keep the signature: def notifier_for(channel).
Branch on the channel: channel == "email".
Return EmailNotifier.new for email.
Fall back to PushNotifier.new.

steps.success: One method, all the branching — that's a factory method.

md: ## Abstract Factory: build FAMILIES of related objects
**Problem:** you need a *matched set* of objects that must be used together — mixing them would be a bug.

**PawWalk scenario:** for payments you need a charger AND a refunder that belong to the same provider. Stripe's charger with PayPal's refunder is nonsense. An abstract factory hands you a coherent pair.
In Ruby the 'factory' is often just a method returning a small object (a `Struct` or `OpenStruct`) that exposes the related methods.

steps.title: playground/abstract_factory.rb
steps.source:
StripeKit = Struct.new(:x) do
  def charge(cents)  = "stripe charge #{cents}"
  def refund(cents)  = "stripe refund #{cents}"
end
PaypalKit = Struct.new(:x) do
  def charge(cents)  = "paypal charge #{cents}"
  def refund(cents)  = "paypal refund #{cents}"
end

def payment_kit(provider)
  provider == "paypal" ? PaypalKit.new : StripeKit.new
end

kit = payment_kit("stripe")
kit.charge(3000)   # => "stripe charge 3000"
kit.refund(3000)   # => "stripe refund 3000"  (same family, guaranteed)
steps.caption: One factory call yields a matched charge/refund pair.

steps.q: How does Abstract Factory differ from Factory Method?
choices: It produces a family of related objects, not just one
It is faster
It requires threads
It only works with Structs
steps.explain: Factory Method makes one product; Abstract Factory makes a consistent GROUP that belong together.
steps.nudge: Think 'matched set', not 'single item'.

steps.title: Return a matched payment kit
prompt: Finish payment_kit so "paypal" returns PaypalKit.new and everything else returns StripeKit.new.
steps.solution:
def payment_kit(provider)
  if provider == "paypal"
    PaypalKit.new
  else
    StripeKit.new
  end
end

checks.hint: Keep the signature: def payment_kit(provider).
Branch on provider == "paypal".
Return PaypalKit.new for paypal.
Otherwise return StripeKit.new.

steps.success: One call, one coherent family of objects.

md: ## Builder: assemble a complex object step by step
**Problem:** an object has many optional parts, and stuffing them all into one constructor is unreadable.
**PawWalk scenario:** a booking search query with filters, a sort, and a page. You want to add pieces fluently and then produce a plain hash to run.
The Ruby realization: chainable methods that each mutate state and `return self`, plus a final `build` that hands back the assembled hash.

steps.title: playground/builder.rb
steps.caption: Each .where returns self, so the calls chain into one expression.

steps.q: What must .where return for the fluent chain .where(...).where(...) to work?
choices: self (the builder itself)
nil
the filters hash
a new QueryBuilder each time
steps.explain: Returning self lets the next method call land on the same builder — that's the chain.
steps.nudge: The next call in the chain needs a builder to call on.

steps.title: Make .where chainable
prompt: Finish .where so it stores value under key in the filters hash AND returns self.

checks.hint: Store it: @filters[key] = value.
Then return self on the next line so it chains.

mustNot.hint: Store the value before returning self.

steps.success: Store, then return self — the fluent builder in two lines.

md: ## Prototype: create new objects by cloning an existing one
**Problem:** building a fresh object from scratch is expensive or fiddly, but you already have a good example to copy.
**PawWalk scenario:** a recurring-booking *template*. Duplicate it, change the date on the copy, and the original template stays untouched for next week.
Ruby gives you this for free: `dup` (shallow copy, ignores frozen/singleton state) and `clone` (also copies frozen state). No `Prototype` interface required.

steps.title: playground/prototype.rb
steps.source:
class BookingTemplate
  attr_accessor :walker, :date
  def initialize(walker, date)
    @walker = walker
    @date   = date
  end
end

template = BookingTemplate.new("Sam", "2026-07-06")
copy = template.dup            # shallow clone of the object
copy.date = "2026-07-13"       # tweak only the copy

template.date  # => "2026-07-06"  (original untouched)
copy.date      # => "2026-07-13"
steps.caption: dup makes an independent copy; mutating it leaves the original alone.

steps.q: After copy = template.dup then copy.date = new_date, what is template.date?
choices: Unchanged — dup made an independent object
Also the new date
nil
It raises FrozenError
steps.explain: dup copies the object, so assigning to the copy's date never touches the original.
steps.nudge: Prototype's promise is that the original is left intact.

steps.title: Clone a template and tweak the copy
prompt: Duplicate template into copy, then set copy.date to "2026-07-13" without touching the original.
steps.starter:
template = BookingTemplate.new("Sam", "2026-07-06")
# your code here
steps.solution:
template = BookingTemplate.new("Sam", "2026-07-06")
copy = template.dup
copy.date = "2026-07-13"

checks.hint: Clone it: copy = template.dup.
Change only the copy: copy.date = "2026-07-13".

mustNot.hint: Don't reassign the original's date.

steps.success: dup gave you an independent object to tweak.

md: ## Adapter: make a foreign interface fit yours
**Problem:** a third-party object exposes the wrong method names, but you don't want that leaking through your whole codebase.
**PawWalk scenario:** a geocoder gem returns `lat_lng`, but your code everywhere calls `.coordinates`. Wrap the gem in an adapter that exposes `coordinates` and delegates.
The adapter owns the vocabulary translation so the rest of your app speaks only *your* interface.

steps.title: playground/adapter.rb
steps.source:
# Foreign gem — we can't change it:
class VendorGeocoder
  def lat_lng(address) = [30.27, -97.74]   # Austin
end

class GeocoderAdapter
  def initialize(vendor) = @vendor = vendor

  def coordinates(address)      # our interface
    @vendor.lat_lng(address)    # delegate to theirs
  end
end

geo = GeocoderAdapter.new(VendorGeocoder.new)
geo.coordinates("Austin, TX")   # => [30.27, -97.74]
steps.caption: coordinates is our name; it forwards to the vendor's lat_lng.

steps.q: What is the adapter's job here?
choices: Translate our .coordinates call into the vendor's .lat_lng
Cache the coordinates
Add authentication
Run the vendor code on a thread
steps.explain: An adapter maps the interface your code expects onto the foreign one it wraps.
steps.nudge: Same data, different method name — who fixes that mismatch?

steps.title: Delegate coordinates to lat_lng
prompt: Finish coordinates so it calls lat_lng(address) on the wrapped @vendor.
steps.starter:
class GeocoderAdapter
  def initialize(vendor) = @vendor = vendor

  def coordinates(address)
    # your code here
  end
end

checks.hint: Expose your name: def coordinates(address).
Delegate: @vendor.lat_lng(address).

steps.success: The mismatch lives in one place now — the adapter.

md: ## Bridge: split abstraction from implementation
**Problem:** two dimensions vary independently. If you subclass for every combination you get a class explosion.
**PawWalk scenario:** a `Notification` (the *what* — reminder, receipt) holds a `Transport` (the *how* — SMS, email). Add a new transport OR a new notification type without touching the other axis.
The bridge is just composition: the abstraction holds a reference to the implementation and delegates the varying part to it.

steps.title: playground/bridge.rb
steps.caption: Notification varies independently of the injected transport.

steps.q: Why is Bridge better than subclassing SmsReminder, EmailReminder, SmsReceipt, EmailReceipt?
choices: It avoids a class per combination — the two axes stay separate
It runs faster
It removes the need for objects
It forces a single transport
steps.explain: Composition over inheritance: N notifications x M transports become N + M classes, not N*M.
steps.nudge: Count the classes for 5 types x 5 transports each way.

steps.title: Delegate send_it to the transport
prompt: Finish send_it so it calls deliver(text) on the injected @transport.
steps.starter:
class Notification
  def initialize(transport) = @transport = transport
  def send_it(text)
    # your code here
  end
end

checks.hint: Keep the signature: def send_it(text).
Delegate across the bridge: @transport.deliver(text).

steps.success: The two axes now grow independently.

md: ## Composite: treat parts and wholes uniformly
**Problem:** you have a tree — items inside groups inside groups — and you want one operation to work on a leaf and a branch the same way.
**PawWalk scenario:** a walk *package* made of individual items and sub-packages. `total_price` should work whether you call it on one item or the whole nested bundle.
The trick: both a leaf and a group answer the same method. A group's answer sums its children (which may themselves be groups).

steps.title: playground/composite.rb
steps.source:
class Item
  def initialize(price) = @price = price
  def total_price       = @price
end

class Package
  def initialize = @children = []
  def add(child)
    @children << child
    self
  end
  def total_price
    @children.sum(&:total_price)   # recurses through nested packages
  end
end

bundle = Package.new
bundle.add(Item.new(2000)).add(Item.new(500))
inner = Package.new.add(Item.new(1000))
bundle.add(inner)
bundle.total_price   # => 3500
steps.caption: Item and Package share total_price; the group just sums its children.

steps.q: How does a Package compute total_price?
choices: It sums total_price of each child, which may itself be a Package
It only counts direct Items
It ignores nested packages
It multiplies the children
steps.explain: Because every child answers total_price, summing them recurses through the whole tree uniformly.
steps.nudge: The children respond to the same method the whole does.

steps.title: Sum the children
prompt: Finish Package#total_price so it sums total_price across every child.
steps.starter:
class Package
  def initialize = @children = []
  def add(child)
    @children << child
    self
  end
  def total_price
    # your code here
  end
end

checks.hint: Define the shared method: def total_price.
Sum uniformly: @children.sum(&:total_price).

steps.success: Leaf and branch answer the same call — that's composite.

md: ## Decorator: wrap an object to add behavior
**Problem:** you want to add responsibilities to a single object without subclassing or editing its class.
**PawWalk scenario:** a base walk price, then optional GPS and insurance surcharges. Wrap the price object; the wrapper's `total` adds its surcharge to the wrapped `total`.
Ruby's `SimpleDelegator` forwards everything to the wrapped object so you override only the method you're changing — but a plain wrapper class works just as well.

steps.title: playground/decorator.rb
steps.source:
BasePrice = Struct.new(:cents) { def total = cents }

class GpsSurcharge
  def initialize(inner) = @inner = inner
  def total             = @inner.total + 300   # adds to the wrapped total
end

class InsuranceSurcharge
  def initialize(inner) = @inner = inner
  def total             = @inner.total + 500
end

price = InsuranceSurcharge.new(GpsSurcharge.new(BasePrice.new(2000)))
price.total   # => 2800  (2000 + 300 + 500)
steps.caption: Each decorator wraps the previous total and adds its own surcharge.

steps.q: What does a decorator's total do with the wrapped object?
choices: Calls the wrapped total and adds its own surcharge
Replaces the wrapped total entirely
Ignores the wrapped object
Mutates the base price object
steps.explain: A decorator delegates to the inner object and layers extra behavior on top of the result.
steps.nudge: It adds to what's inside, it doesn't discard it.

steps.title: Add a surcharge on top
prompt: Finish GpsSurcharge#total so it returns the wrapped @inner.total plus 300.
steps.starter:
class GpsSurcharge
  def initialize(inner) = @inner = inner
  def total
    # your code here
  end
end
steps.solution:
class GpsSurcharge
  def initialize(inner) = @inner = inner
  def total
    @inner.total + 300
  end
end

checks.hint: Wrap and add: @inner.total + 300.

mustNot.hint: Add to the inner total, don't hardcode 300.

steps.success: Behavior added without touching the wrapped class.

md: ## Facade: one simple entry point over a messy subsystem
**Problem:** doing one useful thing requires orchestrating several objects in the right order, and every caller repeats that dance.
**PawWalk scenario:** booking a walk means pricing, then payment, then notification. `BookingService.book!` hides all three behind one call.
A facade doesn't add new logic — it just provides a friendly front door so callers don't wire the subsystem themselves.

steps.title: playground/facade.rb
steps.caption: One method sequences three subsystems and returns a tidy result.

steps.q: What does a Facade add to the subsystem it fronts?
choices: Nothing new — just a simpler, single entry point
A cache layer
Retries
A database
steps.explain: A facade's value is simplification: it orchestrates existing pieces behind one call, adding no new behavior.
steps.nudge: It's a front door, not a new room.

steps.title: Sequence the subsystem
prompt: Finish book! so it quotes with @pricer, charges @pay with those cents, then returns { cents: cents, status: "booked" }.

checks.hint: Quote first: cents = @pricer.quote(mins).
Then charge: @pay.charge(cents).
Return { cents: cents, status: "booked" }.

steps.success: One friendly call over three moving parts.

md: ## Flyweight: share immutable objects to save memory
**Problem:** you'd otherwise create thousands of identical, unchanging objects — wasting memory.
**PawWalk scenario:** thousands of `Dog` records, but only a few dozen breeds. Intern each `Breed` once and share the single instance across every dog of that breed.
The realization is a cache: a hash keyed by the intrinsic value, returning the SAME object every time.

steps.title: playground/flyweight.rb
steps.caption: Repeated calls for the same key return the one shared instance.

steps.q: What makes a Flyweight save memory?
choices: Repeated requests for the same key return the SAME shared instance
It compresses the objects
It stores objects on disk
It deletes objects after use
steps.explain: By sharing one immutable instance per key, thousands of references point at a handful of objects.
steps.nudge: How many Corgi objects exist after 10,000 Corgis?

steps.title: Cache one instance per name
prompt: Finish BreedFactory.get so it memoizes a Breed per name in @pool (build it only if missing).

checks.hint: Memoize: @pool[name] ||= Breed.new(name).

steps.success: One object per key — the fly is now a flyweight.

md: ## Proxy: a stand-in that controls access
**Problem:** you want to gate, delay, or guard access to a real object — lazy loading, auth, rate limiting — without changing that object.
**PawWalk scenario:** a walker's high-res photo is expensive to load. A proxy loads it only on first read, then delegates to the real photo afterward.
The proxy shares the real object's interface but wraps the call with its own logic (here: memoized lazy load).

steps.title: playground/proxy.rb
steps.source:
class RealPhoto
  def initialize(url) = (@url = url; @bytes = load!)
  def load!           = "bytes(#{@url})"     # pretend this is slow
  def data            = @bytes
end

class PhotoProxy
  def initialize(url) = @url = url
  def data
    @real ||= RealPhoto.new(@url)   # load only on first access
    @real.data
  end
end

p = PhotoProxy.new("walker.jpg")   # nothing loaded yet
p.data                              # => "bytes(walker.jpg)"  (loads now)
steps.caption: The proxy defers the expensive RealPhoto until data is first read.

steps.q: When does PhotoProxy build the RealPhoto?
choices: Lazily, on the first call to data
Immediately in initialize
Never
On every call to data
steps.explain: The ||= memoizes: the real object is created on first access and reused after.
steps.nudge: Look at where @real is assigned.

steps.title: Lazily delegate to the real subject
prompt: Finish PhotoProxy#data: build @real (a RealPhoto for @url) only if it's missing, then return @real.data.
steps.starter:
class PhotoProxy
  def initialize(url) = @url = url
  def data
    # your code here
  end
end
steps.solution:
class PhotoProxy
  def initialize(url) = @url = url
  def data
    @real ||= RealPhoto.new(@url)
    @real.data
  end
end

checks.hint: Lazy-load: @real ||= RealPhoto.new(@url).
Then delegate: @real.data.

steps.success: Access controlled, real object untouched.

md: ## Chain of Responsibility: pass a request down handlers
**Problem:** several checks must run in order, and the first failure should stop the rest.
**PawWalk scenario:** a booking validation pipeline — availability, then payment, then policy. If availability fails, don't bother charging.
In Ruby the whole chain is often just an array of callables you run until one returns a failure. No linked-list of handler objects required.

steps.title: playground/chain.rb
steps.caption: Each handler returns nil to pass or a message to stop the chain.

steps.q: In this chain, what does a handler return to let the request continue?
choices: nil (no failure)
true
the request
a new handler
steps.explain: A nil means 'I have no objection'; a non-nil failure short-circuits the chain.
steps.nudge: The loop returns early only when the handler gives back something truthy.

steps.title: Stop at the first failure
prompt: Finish validate: for each handler, call it with req; return the result immediately if it's a failure (truthy). Return nil if all pass.
steps.starter:
def validate(req, handlers)
  handlers.each do |h|
    # your code here
  end
  nil
end
steps.solution:
def validate(req, handlers)
  handlers.each do |h|
    failure = h.call(req)
    return failure if failure
  end
  nil
end

checks.hint: Run the handler: failure = h.call(req).
Short-circuit: return failure if failure.

steps.success: First failure wins — the chain stops early.

md: ## Command: package a request as an object
**Problem:** you want to store, queue, log, or undo an action — which means the action must be a *thing*, not just a method call.
**PawWalk scenario:** background jobs. Each job IS a command: an object responding to `call`. A runner executes a list of them in order.
In Ruby, 'responds to call' is the whole contract — an object with a `call` method, a lambda, or a `Proc` all qualify.

steps.title: playground/command.rb
steps.source:
class SendReceipt
  def initialize(email) = @email = email
  def call              = "receipt -> #{@email}"
end

class ChargeCard
  def initialize(cents) = @cents = cents
  def call              = "charged #{@cents}"
end

def run_all(commands)
  commands.map(&:call)   # each command just responds to call
end

run_all([ChargeCard.new(3000), SendReceipt.new("a@b.com")])
# => ["charged 3000", "receipt -> a@b.com"]
steps.caption: Every command answers call; the runner treats them uniformly.

steps.q: What is the single method a Command must respond to here?
choices: call
execute!
run
perform_later
steps.explain: The runner does commands.map(&:call), so every command just needs a call method.
steps.nudge: Look at what run_all sends to each element.

steps.title: Run a list of commands
prompt: Finish run_all so it returns an array of the result of call on each command.

checks.hint: Keep the signature: def run_all(commands).
Execute each: commands.map(&:call).

steps.success: Actions became objects — now they queue, log, and replay.

md: ## Interpreter: evaluate sentences in a tiny language
**Problem:** you have a small grammar — rules, formulas, filters — and want to evaluate expressions written in it.
> Be honest: full Interpreter is rare in app code. Most 'tiny languages' are better served by data + a fold, or a real parser gem. But the idea is worth knowing.
**PawWalk scenario:** a minimal price-adjustment rule: a list of `["add", n]` / `["sub", n]` tokens applied to a starting total.

steps.title: playground/interpreter.rb
steps.source:
def evaluate(tokens, start)
  tokens.reduce(start) do |total, (op, n)|
    case op
    when "add" then total + n
    when "sub" then total - n
    else            total
    end
  end
end

program = [["add", 500], ["sub", 200], ["add", 100]]
evaluate(program, 2000)   # => 2400
steps.caption: reduce folds the token 'program' over a running total.

steps.q: How does this interpreter evaluate the token list?
choices: It folds (reduce) each token over a running total
It compiles to machine code
It sends each token to a database
It ignores the order of tokens
steps.explain: Each token transforms the accumulator; reduce applies them left to right — a tiny expression evaluator.
steps.nudge: Order matters and there's an accumulator — which Enumerable method is that?

steps.title: Interpret an add/sub program
prompt: Finish evaluate: reduce tokens starting from start; "add" adds n, "sub" subtracts n, anything else leaves total unchanged.
steps.starter:
def evaluate(tokens, start)
  tokens.reduce(start) do |total, (op, n)|
    # your code here
  end
end
steps.solution:
def evaluate(tokens, start)
  tokens.reduce(start) do |total, (op, n)|
    case op
    when "add" then total + n
    when "sub" then total - n
    else            total
    end
  end
end

checks.hint: Add branch: when "add" then total + n.
Sub branch: when "sub" then total - n.

steps.success: A tiny language, evaluated by a fold.

md: ## Iterator: step through a collection without exposing internals
**Problem:** callers want to walk your collection in order without knowing whether it's an array, a tree, or a linked list inside.
> Say it plainly: in Ruby this is nearly built in. Define `each`, `include Enumerable`, and you get `map`, `select`, `sort`, `to_a`, and dozens more for free.
**PawWalk scenario:** iterate a booking calendar in order — callers just call `.each` or `.map` and never see the storage.

steps.title: playground/iterator.rb
steps.source:
class Calendar
  include Enumerable
  def initialize(days) = @days = days
  def each(&block)
    @days.each(&block)   # yield each item; Enumerable builds the rest
  end
end

cal = Calendar.new(["Mon", "Tue", "Wed"])
cal.map(&:upcase)        # => ["MON", "TUE", "WED"]  (free from Enumerable)
cal.select { |d| d != "Tue" }   # => ["Mon", "Wed"]
steps.caption: One each method plus Enumerable = a full iterator toolkit.

steps.q: What do you get by include Enumerable and defining each?
choices: map, select, sort, to_a and dozens more, for free
Only each
Thread safety
A database connection
steps.explain: Enumerable builds every collection method on top of the single each you provide.
steps.nudge: That's the whole reason to include the module.

steps.title: Make Calendar enumerable
prompt: Finish each so it yields every item in @days (pass the block through with &block).
steps.starter:
class Calendar
  include Enumerable
  def initialize(days) = @days = days
  def each(&block)
    # your code here
  end
end
steps.solution:
class Calendar
  include Enumerable
  def initialize(days) = @days = days
  def each(&block)
    @days.each(&block)
  end
end

checks.hint: Accept the block: def each(&block).
Yield each item: @days.each(&block).

steps.success: One method, and the whole Enumerable API lit up.

md: ## Mediator: a hub that coordinates peers
**Problem:** many objects need to talk to each other. Wire them directly and you get an N-to-N tangle where everyone references everyone.
**PawWalk scenario:** a dispatch center. Walker, owner, and tracker don't hold references to each other — they notify the mediator, which routes to the others.
The mediator centralizes the 'who tells whom' logic so the peers stay decoupled.

steps.title: playground/mediator.rb
steps.source:
class Dispatch
  def initialize = @peers = {}
  def register(name, peer) = @peers[name] = peer

  def notify(sender, event)
    @peers.each do |name, peer|
      peer.receive(event) unless name == sender   # everyone but the sender
    end
  end
end

Peer = Struct.new(:log) do
  def receive(event) = log << event
end

hub = Dispatch.new
owner = Peer.new([]); tracker = Peer.new([])
hub.register(:owner, owner)
hub.register(:tracker, tracker)
hub.notify(:owner, "walk_started")
tracker.log   # => ["walk_started"]
steps.caption: notify routes an event to every peer except the sender.

steps.q: What does the Mediator prevent among its peers?
choices: Direct references between every pair of peers (an N-to-N tangle)
Any communication at all
Using structs
Logging
steps.explain: Peers talk to the mediator, not each other, collapsing N-to-N wiring into N-to-1.
steps.nudge: Count the connections with and without the hub.

steps.title: Route to the other peers
prompt: Finish notify: for each registered name/peer, call peer.receive(event) unless that name equals sender.

checks.hint: Route to others: peer.receive(event) unless name == sender.

steps.success: Peers stay decoupled; the hub does the routing.

md: ## Memento: capture state to restore later
**Problem:** you want undo — snapshot an object's state now, and put it back later — without exposing the object's internals to whoever holds the snapshot.
**PawWalk scenario:** a booking *draft*. Snapshot it before risky edits; on cancel, restore the snapshot.
The memento is just a copy of the fields. `save_state` returns it; `restore` copies it back.

steps.title: playground/memento.rb
steps.source:
class BookingDraft
  attr_accessor :walker, :mins
  def initialize(walker, mins) = (@walker = walker; @mins = mins)

  def save_state = { walker: @walker, mins: @mins }   # the memento

  def restore(memento)
    @walker = memento[:walker]
    @mins   = memento[:mins]
  end
end

draft = BookingDraft.new("Sam", 30)
snapshot = draft.save_state
draft.mins = 60                      # risky edit
draft.restore(snapshot)              # undo
draft.mins   # => 30
steps.caption: save_state captures the fields; restore puts them back.

steps.q: What does save_state return in this memento implementation?
choices: A copy of the object's fields to restore later
The object itself
nil
A new BookingDraft
steps.explain: The memento is a snapshot of the fields; restore reads them back into the object.
steps.nudge: It's the thing you hand to restore later.

steps.title: Restore from a memento
prompt: Finish restore so it copies walker and mins out of the memento hash back into the object's ivars.

checks.hint: Restore walker: @walker = memento[:walker].
Restore mins: @mins = memento[:mins].

steps.success: Snapshot and restore — undo in two methods.

md: ## Observer: subscribers get notified on change
**Problem:** when one object changes, several others need to react — but the subject shouldn't hardcode who they are.
**PawWalk scenario:** a walk status flips to `completed`. Notify the owner, analytics, and push — each subscribed independently.
Ruby has `require "observer"; include Observable`, but a plain array of callbacks is often clearer: `subscribe` appends, `notify` calls each.

steps.title: playground/observer.rb
steps.caption: subscribe registers a callback; notify calls every one.

steps.q: Why does the subject keep a list of callbacks instead of naming its observers directly?
choices: So observers can subscribe/unsubscribe without the subject knowing them
To run faster
To avoid using blocks
To store them in a database
steps.explain: The subject stays decoupled: it just calls whatever subscribed, whoever that turns out to be.
steps.nudge: The subject shouldn't need to import the analytics module.

steps.title: Notify every subscriber
prompt: Finish notify so it calls each subscriber callback with status (use .call).
steps.starter:
class Walk
  def initialize = @subscribers = []
  def subscribe(&callback) = @subscribers << callback
  def notify(status)
    # your code here
  end
end

checks.hint: Fan out: @subscribers.each { |cb| cb.call(status) }.

steps.success: Change once, notify many — decoupled.

md: ## State: behavior changes with internal state
**Problem:** an object behaves differently depending on which state it's in, and only certain transitions are legal.
**PawWalk scenario:** a booking machine — `requested -> confirmed -> in_progress -> completed`, with `cancelled` reachable from a few states. Illegal jumps must be rejected.
A clean Ruby realization: a map of allowed transitions and a `transition` method that consults it, raising on an illegal move.

steps.title: playground/state.rb
steps.source:
ALLOWED = {
  "requested"   => ["confirmed", "cancelled"],
  "confirmed"   => ["in_progress", "cancelled"],
  "in_progress" => ["completed"],
  "completed"   => [],
  "cancelled"   => [],
}

class Booking
  attr_reader :state
  def initialize = @state = "requested"

  def transition(to)
    unless ALLOWED[@state].include?(to)
      raise "illegal: #{@state} -> #{to}"
    end
    @state = to
  end
end

b = Booking.new
b.transition("confirmed")     # ok
b.transition("in_progress")   # ok
# b.transition("requested")   # => raises "illegal: in_progress -> requested"
steps.caption: The transition map is the state machine; transition enforces it.

steps.q: What does transition do when the target isn't in the allowed list for the current state?
choices: Raises — the move is illegal
Silently ignores it
Resets to requested
Allows it anyway
steps.explain: Only transitions listed in the map are legal; anything else raises, which is the whole safety point.
steps.nudge: The unless-guard leads to a raise.

steps.title: Guard the transition
prompt: Finish transition: unless ALLOWED[@state] includes to, raise an error; otherwise set @state = to.
steps.starter:
class Booking
  attr_reader :state
  def initialize = @state = "requested"
  def transition(to)
    # your code here
  end
end
steps.solution:
class Booking
  attr_reader :state
  def initialize = @state = "requested"
  def transition(to)
    unless ALLOWED[@state].include?(to)
      raise "illegal"
    end
    @state = to
  end
end

checks.hint: Guard: unless ALLOWED[@state].include?(to).
Reject illegal moves with raise.
On success: @state = to.

steps.success: Only legal moves get through — a real state machine.

md: ## Strategy: swap interchangeable algorithms at runtime
**Problem:** one operation has several interchangeable implementations, chosen per call.
**PawWalk scenario:** pricing — flat, surge, or member-discount — picked per booking.
> Say it plainly: in Ruby a strategy is usually just a lambda or any callable. You don't need a `Strategy` class hierarchy; you inject a block.

steps.title: playground/strategy.rb
steps.caption: The strategy is just an injected callable; price delegates to it.

steps.q: In idiomatic Ruby, what is a Strategy most often?
choices: A lambda or any callable you inject
A subclass of Strategy
A frozen constant
A database row
steps.explain: Ruby's first-class callables mean the strategy is usually just a lambda, no class hierarchy needed.
steps.nudge: First-class functions dissolve the pattern.

steps.title: Apply the injected strategy
prompt: Finish price so it calls the injected @strategy with base and returns the result.
steps.starter:
class PriceCalculator
  def initialize(strategy) = @strategy = strategy
  def price(base)
    # your code here
  end
end

checks.hint: Keep the signature: def price(base).
Delegate to the strategy: @strategy.call(base).

steps.success: Swap the lambda, swap the algorithm.

md: ## Template Method: base defines the skeleton, subclass fills a step
**Problem:** several variants share the same overall flow but differ in one or two steps.
**PawWalk scenario:** a report generator whose `run` always does header, body, footer. Each report type overrides only `body`.

The base method calls overridable methods; subclasses override just the varying step. (In Ruby you could also inject a block — but the classic template method is a clean fit when the skeleton is fixed.)

steps.title: playground/template_method.rb
steps.source:
class ReportSection
  def run
    [header, body, footer].join("\n")   # the fixed skeleton
  end
  def header = "== Report =="
  def body   = "(empty)"                 # meant to be overridden
  def footer = "== End =="
end

class WalksReport < ReportSection
  def body = "walks: 42"
end

WalksReport.new.run
# => "== Report ==\nwalks: 42\n== End =="
steps.caption: run is the template; subclasses override only body.

steps.q: Which part does the subclass override in Template Method?
choices: Only the varying step(s), not the overall skeleton
The entire run method
The constructor
Nothing
steps.explain: The base fixes the sequence in run; subclasses change just the steps that differ, like body.
steps.nudge: The skeleton stays put; the filling changes.

steps.title: Override one step
prompt: Make WalksReport subclass ReportSection and override body to return "walks: 42".
steps.starter:
class WalksReport < ReportSection
  # your code here
end
steps.solution:
class WalksReport < ReportSection
  def body = "walks: 42"
end

checks.hint: Subclass it: class WalksReport < ReportSection.
Override the step: def body = "walks: 42".

mustNot.hint: Leave run to the base class — override only body.

steps.success: One overridden step, whole skeleton reused.

md: ## Visitor: add operations over a fixed structure via double dispatch
**Problem:** you have a fixed set of node types and keep needing *new operations* over them — and you don't want to edit every node class each time.
**PawWalk scenario:** booking line items. A `Total` visitor sums them; a `Receipt` visitor formats them. Add a new visitor without touching the items.

> Be honest: Visitor is clunky in dynamic languages. In real Ruby you'd often replace it with a `case` on type or pattern matching. It shines only when the node set is truly fixed and operations grow often.

steps.title: playground/visitor.rb
steps.source:
class LineItem
  attr_reader :cents
  def initialize(cents) = @cents = cents
  def accept(visitor)   = visitor.visit_item(self)   # double dispatch
end

class TotalVisitor
  def initialize      = @sum = 0
  def visit_item(item) = @sum += item.cents
  attr_reader :sum
end

items = [LineItem.new(2000), LineItem.new(500)]
totaler = TotalVisitor.new
items.each { |i| i.accept(totaler) }
totaler.sum   # => 2500
steps.caption: accept calls back into visit_item — the double dispatch handshake.

steps.q: What is the 'double dispatch' in Visitor?
choices: item.accept(visitor) then visitor.visit_item(item) — two calls select behavior by both types
Calling accept twice
Using two threads
Two inheritance levels
steps.explain: The node's accept picks the right visit_x, so the operation is chosen by BOTH the node type and the visitor.
steps.nudge: Two method calls, each picking on a different type.

steps.title: Wire up accept
prompt: Finish LineItem#accept so it calls visit_item(self) on the visitor.
steps.starter:
class LineItem
  attr_reader :cents
  def initialize(cents) = @cents = cents
  def accept(visitor)
    # your code here
  end
end
steps.solution:
class LineItem
  attr_reader :cents
  def initialize(cents) = @cents = cents
  def accept(visitor)
    visitor.visit_item(self)
  end
end

checks.hint: Keep the signature: def accept(visitor).
Double dispatch: visitor.visit_item(self).

steps.success: accept hands 'self' back to the visitor — dispatch complete.
